title: Can the AI Industry Regulate Itself? Stripe Wants PayPal, China Catches Up, NY Bans Datacenters

summary.tldr: The All-In crew debates Demis Hassabis's proposal for a FINRA-style self-regulatory organization (SRO) for AI, which Sacks conditionally endorses over a government agency while renewing his attack on Anthropic for pursuing regulatory capture. Other threads cover the Stripe/Block/Advent bid for PayPal as the start of a wave of 'AI-ify the legacy business' megadeals, the massive cost gap between frontier and open/Chinese models, and NY's data center moratorium framed as foreign-influenced moral panic. Closes with a striking science segment on an AlphaFold-designed enzyme that reversed aged human skin to a 31-year-old's profile.

topics.title: AI self-regulation via an SRO
topics.detail: Hassabis proposed a US-led, industry-funded self-regulatory body modeled on FINRA: frontier labs submit models 30 days pre-release, benchmarks updated quarterly, initially voluntary then mandatory. Sacks says he could 'potentially get on board' with five conditions: broad representation (startups/open source, not just the three big labs), review only true frontier models, focus only on catastrophic cyber/CBRN risk (not speech/disinfo), keep it voluntary first, and make it a substitute for a new agency, not additive. He warns Dario's 'FAA for AI' would take years to certify models (Boeing 737 MAX took ~5 years) and lose the race to China.

topics.title: Anthropic and regulatory capture
topics.detail: Sacks doubles down on his October 2024 claim that Anthropic runs a 'sophisticated regulatory capture strategy based on fear mongering,' citing a Politico report that Anthropic pushes states toward progressively stricter, non-uniform AI rules (starting with California's SB 53). He notes Anthropic is now ~$1T valued (Gavin Baker predicts $3T post-IPO) and is even funding NGOs like 'public first' that oppose data center construction\u2014despite compute being the main constraint on its own revenue.

topics.title: Stripe/Block/Advent bid for PayPal
topics.detail: Stripe and Advent (with Block reportedly contributing $17B equity) bid ~$53-60B / ~$60/share for PayPal (peak market cap was $322B). Chamath argues the real play is building a Visa/MasterCard competitor by combining PayPal's ~439M consumer accounts, Stripe's merchant rails (~$2T annual volume vs PayPal's ~$1.7T), stablecoin infra (Bridge + PYUSD) and Block's point-of-sale. Both predict a wave of 'AI-native operators reviving stale digital businesses' (eBay/Ryan Cohen, Bending Spoons rolling up AOL, Vimeo, Evernote), and hint Elon could counter-bid. Chamath expects the final price 10-15% higher.

topics.title: Token costs, data leaks and the open-model ecosystem
topics.detail: Chamath cites input-token prices ranging from ~$56/M (Fable) down to Chinese models at ~50 cents/M, with Grok ~$1-1.50 and Chinese models 50c; Ramp launched token-spend management after seeing 21x growth in token spend. He and Sacks argue enterprises are 'double whammy' overpaying while leaking proprietary 'alpha' data\u2014illustrated by xAI's Grok build inadvertently uploading users' entire codebases before being shut off July 13. J-Cal claims Apple could be a 'screaming buy' as M7 Ultra chips supporting up to 1.5TB RAM enable running near-frontier models locally.

topics.title: NY data center moratorium and foreign influence
topics.detail: Gov. Hochul signed the first statewide hyperscale data center moratorium; Sacks rebuts each claim (behind-the-meter power, land efficiency, closed-loop water use \u2014 a data center uses water 'like two and a half In-N-Out burgers,' natural gas is clean-burning). Freeberg presents a chart tying anti-GMO sentiment to Russia Today's 2010-2022 US presence and cites an OpenAI blog ('PRC-linked influence operations are targeting AI debates in the US') to argue foreign scyops drive anti-data-center panic. Chamath warns the US will be ~2.5x California's energy consumption short by 2050, citing a PJM auction that needed 7-8 GW but got ~156 MW.

topics.title: Apple sues OpenAI; science corner on aging reversal
topics.detail: Apple filed a 41-page suit alleging OpenAI (which poached 400+ Apple employees) induced candidates to bring 'actual parts' to interviews and stole trade secrets for its hardware device; the hosts decline to opine on unproven allegations. Freeberg details a Calico/Reveal Pharma paper using AlphaFold plus directed evolution to design a novel enzyme that degrades CML (glycation end-products) in the extracellular matrix, removing 55% of CML from >70-year-old donor skin\u2014reversing it to a 31-year-old's profile. He predicts a trillion-dollar cosmetic market first.

quotes: If my choices are between FAA for AI or what I would call the DMV for AI, I would much rather go for Demis' SRO for AI.
They poke the tiger... it's more than that. They're funding the money for the tiger.
Number one, brand yourself as a safe AI company. Number two, ban unsafe AI. Three, profit.
It's a misnomer to call it the PayPal mafia. It's really the PayPal diaspora. Our homeland was taken over and they burned our temple.
At $56 per million input tokens versus 50 cents for the rest of the world, it doesn't take a company much worse than you to beat you when your cost is 50 to 100X more.
summary.verdict_reason: Sacks's five conditions for AI self-regulation and Chamath's concrete token-economics and PayPal-deal analysis offer genuinely useful investor framing, though the recurring Anthropic-bashing and foreign-scyop theorizing run long and repetitive.

title: Former Intel CEO on What Went Wrong, What's Next + Lovable CEO on the Real Promise of Vibe Coding

summary.tldr: Two interviews: former Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger dissects how Intel lost its lead to Nvidia, TSMC and Apple, and offers a sobering warning about Taiwan's fragility. Then Lovable CEO Anton Osika explains the state of 'vibe coding' and how his 20-month-old company reached $500M+ revenue. Key takeaway: Intel's decline stemmed from business people replacing technologists, while AI-native software creation is collapsing build costs by orders of magnitude.

topics.title: Why Intel went off the rails
topics.detail: Gelsinger blames the shift from technical leadership (Grove, Moore, Noyce, and 15 of 20 exec-staff PhDs) to 'bean counters' and finance people. Intel returned ~$100B to shareholders via dividends and buybacks in the five to six years before he came back, passed on making iPhone chips, and hadn't built a new factory in a decade.

topics.title: How Apple, Nvidia and TSMC won
topics.detail: Steve Jobs quietly prepared Apple silicon by porting macOS to x86 across four prior releases before going in-house. Nvidia's CUDA/GPU stack got 'a little better every release' until HPC and AI adopted it. Intel's competing Larrabee x86 project was killed a week after Gelsinger first left. TSMC's foundry model won, producing 5x Intel's wafers by 2021, now ~7x.

topics.title: Taiwan's terrifying fragility
topics.detail: Gelsinger cites a WSJ article: Taiwan has less than three weeks of energy reserves. A blockade browns out fabs, which don't restart for 90 days, causing economic impact 'greater than the Great Depression' — no shots needed. He notes China has blockaded the Taiwan Strait seven times in four years. US leading-edge production rose from ~12% to ~18% thanks to the CHIPS Act.

topics.title: AI bubble and the energy ceiling
topics.detail: Gelsinger argues energy capacity (growing 4-5% globally, only ~1% in the US for a decade) acts as a natural cap preventing over-building. He predicts a 'couple of decades' buildout, wants to make AI 10,000x cheaper (five orders of magnitude per token), and expects meaningful quantum results before 2030, with encryption-breaking implications by 2032-33.

topics.title: Lovable's growth and vibe coding
topics.detail: Osika reports 1M new products built weekly, 50M+ apps, 700M+ monthly visits, and revenue hitting $500M in May after 20 months. About 20% of users are technical; 80% non-technical. Some customers run businesses making $1M+ revenue. ~60% of customers hit usage caps and pay overages. Pricing starts at $25/month.

topics.title: Model strategy and the future
topics.detail: Lovable routes to multiple frontier and open-weight models, applying reinforcement learning to fix errors frontier models make. It's building a hosting product (an AWS competitor), an 'AI co-founder,' and post-training research in Europe. Osika describes a customer replacing 10+ tools and saving $1M/year, predicting more bespoke software replacing SaaS incumbents.

quotes: One of the things that went off the rail was when it started to be run by business people as opposed to technical.
The island of Taiwan has less than three weeks of energy reserves... the economic impact of a brown out of Taiwan is greater than the Great Depression.
In four to eight hours they made the whole intranet... lovable is 50 bucks a month. I would equate what would have cost me $500,000 two years ago.
People have announced that lovable is dead every six months since you started, and then every six months you go from a hundred to two hundred to three hundred million.
summary.verdict_reason: Gelsinger offers genuinely insider, specific analysis of Intel's decline and a striking Taiwan-energy warning, while the Lovable segment gives concrete metrics on the vibe-coding boom — valuable for anyone tracking semis or AI startups, though the ad reads and softball framing drag it down.

title: The Trillion-Dollar Industries AI Is Disrupting: Voice, Law & the End of the Billable Hour

summary.tldr: Two on-stage All-In interviews with founders riding steep growth curves: Mati Staniszewski of ElevenLabs (voice AI) and Max Junestrand of Legora (legal AI). Both describe how AI is compressing trillion-dollar service industries, their revenue ramps, competition with frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic), and why they build narrow rather than general models. Core takeaway: AI-native startups are pulling revenue out of legacy service markets fast, and incumbents (LexisNexis, Westlaw, big law firms) are structurally slow to adapt.

topics.title: ElevenLabs revenue ramp and structure
topics.detail: ElevenLabs started in 2022, shipped its first human-sounding text-to-speech in early 2023, took ~20 months to reach $100M ARR, 10 months to $200M, 5 months to $300M, and is now at $600M with 600 employees. It runs 5-10 person teams with engineers embedded even in legal, talent, and go-to-market functions; it has no product managers and never did.

topics.title: Voice AI crossing the human-fidelity threshold
topics.detail: Staniszewski argues the last 6-12 months were a step change where interruptible, low-latency voice agents beat human interactions for speed. He cites financial-services clients (Revolut, Klarna, PKO Bank) where people are more willing to disclose payment problems to an AI than a human out of shame, and a shift from reactive to proactive support.

topics.title: Voice as identity: cloning, safeguards, and licensing
topics.detail: ElevenLabs traces all generated audio, moderates voice and text, and offers AI-detection tools. Its voice marketplace has paid over $22M back to voice talent. Deals include Matthew McConaughey (WorldWide), Masterclass interactive instructors (Gordon Ramsay), Headspace localization, and the James Earl Jones/Darth Vader estate deal powering Fortnite's interactive Vader with Disney.

topics.title: Legora's legal-AI growth and the trillion-dollar disruption
topics.detail: Junestrand claims Legora grew ~50% quarter-over-quarter for seven straight quarters and went from $1M to $150M ARR, calling it one of the fastest direct-sales enterprise ramps. He frames legal services as a $1T market with only ~$40B software spend (4% software, 96% services), a supply-constrained gap AI will fill.

topics.title: End of the billable hour and enterprise insourcing
topics.detail: Junestrand says law firms overcharge for associates and undercharge partners (Kirkland can bill up to $4,000/hr); AI threatens that model, pushing fixed-fee and success-fee pricing. Legora acquired four businesses this year doing diligence in-house, closing one deal in 12 days from LOI. It uses 'legal engineers' (like Palantir's forward-deployed engineers) to help firms transition.

topics.title: Data moats, incumbents, and model strategy
topics.detail: Junestrand argues legal AI needs 100% of case law, not the top 80%, requiring physically scanning courthouse books for page citations. He says LexisNexis and Westlaw (a US duopoly) are being crushed and can't pivot fast. Both founders reject building general models: Legora builds narrow fine-tuned models (e.g. for tabular contract extraction) and refuses on-prem deployment; ElevenLabs competes on architecture and proprietary audio data labeled by 1,000+ contractors.

topics.title: Competing with frontier labs
topics.detail: Both spend tens of millions with OpenAI/Anthropic while those labs eye their categories. ElevenLabs stays model-agnostic and acknowledges rivals try to distill its data. Legora says Claude's legal offering (bundled Markdown skills files) actually generates pipeline because users hit its ceiling and then call Legora.

quotes: Roughly 10 months to get to 200, five months to get to 300... and now we are at 600.
I said, what about the cap table? ChatGPT, brah.
It's a trillion dollars every year into legal services... but the software spend into legal technology is about 40 billion. So it means there's 4% software, 96% service, which is bananas.
It's the opposite of the power law. You don't just need the top 80%. You actually need all of it.
We're hosting national secrets, weapons manufacturers, with their contracts on Legora.
summary.verdict_reason: Two founders share concrete revenue numbers, market-sizing, and business-model insights on AI disrupting voice and legal services, though the interviews are promotional and light on hard skepticism.

title: More Trillion Dollar IPOs, Anthropic $3T, Zuck's Price War, China Ends Open Source?, Trump Accounts

summary.tldr: An All-In episode (with guest Brad Gerstner filling much of the airtime) covering the coming wave of trillion-dollar AI IPOs, the debate over whether enterprise AI spend actually delivers ROI, the open-source vs. frontier-model battle, China possibly closing its open models, and a lengthy segment on the newly launched 'Trump accounts' investment program for children. The most important thread: frontier labs (Anthropic, OpenAI) are seeing revenue explode and could IPO this year at over a trillion dollars, but a reckoning on token-cost ROI is coming.

topics.title: Trillion-dollar IPO rush
topics.detail: SpaceX IPO'd raising $75B at a $1.75T valuation on ~$35B forward revenue, now trading ~$2T (up 25%). Anthropic filed confidentially June 1; Gerstner says Anthropic could exit 2025 over $100B revenue and IPO this year at up to $3T, with Altimeter, Fidelity, T-Rowe eager buyers. OpenAI rumored ~$70B run rate but slower to go public due to corporate restructuring.

topics.title: Token spend and the ROI reckoning
topics.detail: Chamath says his portfolio company's token costs are doubling every 45 days while productivity gains are ~5% max, arguing models have 'asymptoted' and enterprises will eventually demand ROI above the risk-free rate. He cited an analysis that AI's actual EPS lift on the S&P 493 was 0-2%. Gerstner counters the TAM is so vast that no one cares yet and revenue could 3-5x again.

topics.title: Open source vs. frontier models
topics.detail: Despite the DeepSeek moment 18 months ago, share of wallet keeps shifting to frontier labs (open source dropped from 19% to 11% of enterprise spend per Sacks) even as cheap-token usage rises. CTOs from Uber, DoorDash, Databricks described routing hard tasks to frontier models and cheap tasks to open models like Kimi and GLM 5.2. Chamath argues intelligence may not converge—recursive self-improvement could widen the frontier lead.

topics.title: China possibly closing its open models
topics.detail: Reuters reported Chinese regulators met with Alibaba, ByteDance and Z.ai about restricting overseas access to top models, citing fears the US could exploit vulnerabilities. Sacks notes the pattern: stay open until you catch the frontier, then close (as Alibaba's Qwen and Zhipu's GLM appear to be doing), mirroring OpenAI's own shift.

topics.title: Trump accounts launch
topics.detail: Gerstner detailed the Invest America Act program: every child gets a $1,000 seed investment account at birth, invested in the S&P 500, that can compound tax-advantaged. The app hit #1 in the App Store, 1.5M accounts created in first 24 hours, over $1B deposited. Michael/Susan Dell put in $6B+, Gwen Shotwell donated SpaceX shares, Gerstner funded all Indiana kids and donated $100M; goal is 50-70M auto-created accounts within 90 days.

quotes: He said right now our token costs are doubling every 45 days... my upside is essentially flat. He said honestly what we're finding out is that you need to use a lot more tokens to get to this next iteration of improvement, because we've effectively already asymptoted.
Two hundred billion of incremental revenue is incomprehensible in the history of Silicon Valley.
The best thing that could happen for America in terms of winning the AI race against China is if China somehow sprouted their own doomer community.
The antidote to more socialism is more capitalism. And as I told the president, this is more capitalism.
The easiest compounding in the world is between zero and 25.
summary.verdict_reason: The AI IPO and token-ROI debate offers genuinely useful, specific insights from someone with insider positioning, though the extended Trump accounts segment is heavily promotional and could be skimmed.

title: Open Source Wins, AGI Is Here, and Scorsese's AI Toolkit with CEOs of Cerebras & Black Forest Labs

summary.tldr: Two-part interview episode. Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman argues the AI buildout is the largest mobilization since WWII, that inference-heavy reasoning is Cerebras's sweet spot, and that AGI has effectively arrived. Black Forest Labs CEO Robin Rombach discusses open-source image/video generation, a Scorsese partnership, and how the same multimodal models will power robotics. The through-line: open source and 'sovereignty' are ascendant, and compute demand is booked years ahead.

topics.title: The AI buildout scale and Cerebras's $25B backlog
topics.detail: Feldman claims coming data centers will use more power in the next several years than Earth consumed in the prior 50, with single buildings drawing more power than mid-sized cities. Cerebras has a $25 billion backlog, and customers (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Google, Microsoft, AWS) are booking chips before they're finished because demand outstrips buildout capacity.

topics.title: Reasoning as inference and Cerebras's Moore's law claim
topics.detail: Feldman argues reasoning models are compute-intensive inference, which favors Cerebras's fast chips (claimed ~15x faster). He says all prior chips followed Moore's law (doubling ~every 18 months); Cerebras broke it and expects 'way over 2x' in the next 18 months due to a newer architecture with more optimization headroom than 20-year-old GPUs.

topics.title: Open source and sovereignty momentum
topics.detail: Both argue open source is having a moment. Cerebras runs GLM, Kimi, Qwen, OpenAI's OSS-120B, and sovereign models for G42/MBZUAI and GSK. Feldman says the US needs more domestic open-source champions since the current choice is OSS-120B or Chinese models, driven by regulated industries (HIPAA, FINRA) wanting on-prem control and data sovereignty.

topics.title: AGI arrival and regulated model rollouts
topics.detail: Feldman asserts AGI is effectively here, having blown past every historical definition including the Turing test. On staged model releases (referencing government intervention on 'Fable/5.6' and Dario Amodei's messaging), he says phased rollout for red-teaming isn't unreasonable, citing Palo Alto's Nikesh Arora finding critical bugs in his own software that took six weeks to patch.

topics.title: Black Forest Labs: multimodal models and Scorsese
topics.detail: Rombach (co-inventor of latent diffusion, ex-Stable Diffusion, creator of Flux) describes converging image/video/audio models with action prediction for robotics. He met Scorsese multiple times, who used the tools to get 'the mental picture out of your head' for an Eastern European village scene, framing AI as a medium for human-in-the-loop iteration rather than full auto-generated movies.

topics.title: Production use, IP, and hiring
topics.detail: Rombach notes generative AI is being used in high-end film production (referencing a $30M Bitcoin movie with Gal Gadot that avoided green screens, allegedly saving ~$120M). BFL blocks certain IP generation on public tools but partners with IP holders. Company crossed 100 people, raised fresh funding, hiring researchers and forward-deployed engineers in Germany and SF.

quotes: We're talking about individual buildings, the size of football fields, that have more power coming into them than mid-sized cities.
We have a $25 billion backlog... they're trying to capture yesterday's demand. The demand is way outstripping our ability to build data centers.
All chips prior to us followed Moore's law. And we broke it... my view is in the next 18 months will be way over two X.
There's a shot that our children, none of them, nor the people they love will die of cancer.
summary.verdict_reason: Two credible operators give concrete numbers on the buildout and open-source shift, but the conversation is largely optimistic and unchallenged with heavy digression, so worth a skim for the Cerebras backlog and Moore's law claims rather than a full listen.

title: AI Sovereignty Wars, Palantir-Nvidia Deal, SCOTUS Birthright Ruling, Newsom's CA Budget Lie

summary.tldr: The All-In crew spends most of the episode on "AI sovereignty"\proprietary data over to frontier labs like Anthropic and OpenAI, arguing enterprises should run open-source models on their own hardware to avoid being commoditized. They also cover the Palantir-Nvidia sovereign AI deal, the birthright citizenship SCOTUS ruling, immigration policy, and a detailed bear case on California's budget. The core takeaway: control your own models, data, and compute or your model provider will eventually compete with you.

topics.title: AI Sovereignty and the Anthropic threat
topics.detail: Sacks argues frontier labs use their model dominance to hoover up customer 'alpha' then compete—citing Anthropic's Claude Design blindsiding Figma (whose stock fell ~50% this year) and launches of Claude Code/Science/Security/Legal/Financial into categories built by their own customers like Cursor. He compares it to Microsoft killing Lotus/WordPerfect and Google keeping searches on-site (fewer than half now kick you off-site).

topics.title: Palantir-Nvidia sovereign AI deal
topics.detail: Palantir will use Nvidia's Nemotron open models to build a 'sovereign AI operating system' where US government agencies own the hardware, data, and model weights. Sacks frames Nvidia (chips) and Palantir (apps) as natural allies wanting a competitive model layer against the emerging Anthropic (~$60B ARR) / OpenAI (~$40B ARR) duopoly.

topics.title: Open-source model economics
topics.detail: Chamath ran 8090's harness on a code-migration task: their harness plus Claude was 1.4x cheaper and 1.5x faster than Opus alone, but wrapping the best open-source model was 16.4x cheaper (though 3x slower). He cites BCG data that half of large US firms can't earn returns above the ~8-11% cost of capital, arguing giving data to a shared provider is now 'derelict and irresponsible.'

topics.title: AI job loss debate
topics.detail: Sharp disagreement: Jason claims customer support, data entry, and driving jobs will vanish fast (Waymo has stopped recruiting drivers in saturated markets); Sacks and Friedberg push back citing a Ramp/Revelio study of 21,000 firms showing heavy AI adopters grew headcount ~10% (entry-level +12%). Consensus lands on 'displacement not loss' plus a premium for humans in the loop, citing Klarna reversing its AI customer-service cuts.

topics.title: SCOTUS birthright citizenship ruling
topics.detail: Court struck down Trump's EO 6-3, with Roberts joined by liberals. Debate on textualist vs. intentionalist reading of the 14th Amendment (originally for freed slaves post-Dred Scott). Friedberg's position: citizenship should extend to children of legal residents/green-card holders but not tourists or visitors.

topics.title: California budget bear case
topics.detail: Friedberg dissects Newsom's $355B 'balanced' budget (up 65% from $215B in 2019), calling it balanced only via $20-40B of borrowing. Top 1% (150,000 people) pay ~$70B of ~$142B personal income tax; top 1,000 pay ~$22B. New 8% software sales tax, health-insurance tax, permanent 14.4% top rate, plus ~$1.5-2T in unfunded pension/healthcare liabilities that sit senior to bonds under the 'California rule.' Predicts eventual default and a union crisis if red states are asked to bail it out.

quotes: Nobody who went to bed with Microsoft in the 80s, Facebook in the 2000s, or Sam Altman now in the 2020s, did not wake up with their throat slit.
You can't rent intelligence from the same place that rents it to your competitor.
What technical customers want is control over their compute, their models, their data stack, and their alpha... they own the means of production.
Leftism is basically just people trying to string together words to justify taking your money.
summary.verdict_reason: The AI sovereignty thread offers a genuinely sharp, data-backed argument about enterprise strategy and the frontier-lab business model, though the immigration and California segments are more familiar opinion territory.

title: The Rise and Fall of the Roman Empire and the Byzantine Empire | Lex Fridman Podcast #498

summary.tldr: Historian Anthony Kaldellis argues that the 'Byzantine Empire' is a modern invention: the Eastern Roman state was simply the Roman Empire continuing unbroken from 753 BC to 1453 AD. The central thesis is that this society survived over a thousand years not through military dictatorship or luck but through a 'monarchical republic' structure where emperors were held accountable by a constant threat of violent overthrow, and were genuinely incentivized to serve their subjects. Kaldellis repeatedly emphasizes taxation as the engine holding the empire together, and argues the empire fell only to external invasions, never internal decay.

topics.title: Byzantium was just Rome, continuously
topics.detail: Kaldellis argues the burden of proof is on those calling it something other than Roman; its subjects called themselves Romans through 1453 and beyond. The full Roman state spanned roughly 2,200 years, and the East-West 'split' of 395 was a conventional date for a gradual process, not a rupture.

topics.title: The monarchical republic and violent accountability
topics.detail: About 46% of Constantinople's emperors were overthrown by violence; roughly 120 civil wars occurred over the thousand years he studies. Kaldellis argues there was no right to the throne, so emperors ran a 'perpetual referendum'—gauging support via crowds in the Hippodrome (capacity ~30,000 to 100,000)—and served subjects well to avoid being deposed, blinded, or killed.

topics.title: Taxation as the core engine
topics.detail: Kaldellis names taxation as the single most important factor in the empire's cohesion and longevity. Diocletian (r. 284-305) created universal census-based taxation, ending Italy's tax exemption; there were no 'isolated' peasant communities because every arable plot was censused and taxed multiple times a year.

topics.title: The Edict of Caracalla (212 AD)
topics.detail: Extended Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants (up from maybe a third), and crucially they 'meant it'—within a generation emperors and top officials were all provincials. Kaldellis contrasts this with the unthinkability of Britain granting citizenship and the throne to colonial subjects.

topics.title: Constantine and Christianity
topics.detail: Kaldellis ranks Constantine his #1 emperor for consequential decisions (founding Constantinople, Christian conversion) despite him murdering his son Crispus and wife. Christianity was maybe 10% of the population under Constantine and took until the 6th century to dominate; Kaldellis argues Rome co-opted Christianity rather than Christianity conquering Rome.

topics.title: Justinian's mixed legacy
topics.detail: A peasant-descended emperor (r. 527-565) who codified Roman law (the Corpus Juris Civilis, still foundational today), rebuilt Hagia Sophia, and reconquered western provinces. But Kaldellis criticizes the overextension: he stripped eastern armies, letting Persia sack Antioch, and ordered the massacre of ~30,000 in the Nika riots.

topics.title: Crises were external, resilience internal
topics.detail: Kaldellis identifies discrete swift catastrophes—Arab conquests (630s), Seljuk conquest of Asia Minor (1070s), Fourth Crusade (1204)—as external shocks, arguing the empire had no internal decay, no separatist movements (except Bulgaria), and always recovered. He dates the point of no return to the 1340s. Also debunks the maximalist 50% mortality claim for the Plague of Justinian and downplays iconoclasm as a concern of only a few hundred elites.

topics.title: Lessons for the modern 'American empire'
topics.detail: Kaldellis argues institutions that visibly serve the majority (with credible rhetoric matching action) benefit rulers long-term. He criticizes the large gap between US foreign-policy rhetoric and action (citing the 'feminist war' framing of Afghanistan), noting Rome's army largely did what emperors claimed it did—protect subjects.

quotes: We're talking about a society that was right in the middle of one of the main corridors of empire building and new religions in the world. This is the most dangerous neighborhood that you can possibly live in.
Something like 46% of the emperors of Constantinople are overthrown through violence... which means that every emperor is vulnerable and insecure, and they know it.
New Rome is literally stealing food from the mouth of Old Rome.
The best way to persuade your subjects that you're legitimate benevolent kings is to be that.
summary.verdict_reason: A lucid, genuinely revisionist deep-dive on why a complex state stayed stable for a millennium—rich in transferable lessons on institutions, incentives, and the rhetoric-versus-action gap—though its relevance to tech and markets is indirect and it runs long.

title: Nate Silver Predicts: Democrats Take the House, Newsom Is Fading & AOC Might Win It All in 2028

summary.tldr: Nate Silver joins All-In for a wide-ranging conversation on the 2026 midterms and 2028 presidential race. His core thesis: partisanship is 'the gravity' that dictates U.S. elections, Democrats are heavy favorites to retake the House, and the party's 2028 field is shifting toward younger, anti-establishment candidates. Silver argues Gavin Newsom is fading while AOC and John Ossoff are rising, and defends California's election integrity while criticizing its slow, overly complex counting system.

topics.title: California elections and fraud claims
topics.detail: Silver says there's 'no evidence of fraud' in LA/California elections; late Democratic surges reflect systematic differences in when partisans vote (Democrats vote by mail and later), not rigging. He calls California's multi-week count 'completely unacceptable' and 'a sign of atrophy,' noting India, France, UK and Japan count within hours, and says he's not against voter ID.

topics.title: Midterm predictions
topics.detail: Prediction markets put Democrats at 80-85% to take the House and 40-45% for the Senate; Silver thinks the House is closer to 85-90%. His model is due in a few weeks. Trump's approval is around 38%; anxiety over gas prices and Iran are key variables that could shift races.

topics.title: Senate math
topics.detail: Democrats must win red-state seats. In Maine, Graham Plattner leads Susan Collins by only 2 points in a D+8/D+10 environment, which Silver calls an 'unnecessary risk.' In Texas, Talarico is in a toss-up against a weak GOP nominee (Paxton). Senate and House odds are 'almost perfectly correlated.'

topics.title: The three Democratic factions
topics.detail: Silver splits Democrats into the left (Mamdani, AOC, Bernie), the 'abundance libs' (Ezra Klein, pro-market centrists critical of California governance), and 'resistance libs' (partisan Biden defenders). He argues Newsom is courting the resistance-lib faction by embracing the Bidens.

topics.title: 2028 outlook: Newsom fading, AOC and Ossoff rising
topics.detail: Newsom has dropped from ~25% to 15% in primary polls and from ~33% to 22% on Polymarket. Silver names John Ossoff as possibly the most electable Democrat (young, progressive record, won a purple state) and says AOC—now 36 and eligible—is his best bet for the burn-it-down/anti-establishment lane.

topics.title: Socialism, capitalism, and generational divides
topics.detail: Silver pegs the attitudinal cut point at roughly age 40 (those who came of age around 9/11). Democratic losses are concentrated among entrepreneurial Hispanic and Asian-American voters. He cites ~45 million college grads in the last decade burdened by student debt as a driver of disillusionment.

topics.title: Social media and algorithms
topics.detail: Silver criticizes X's shift from follower-based to algorithmic feeds (a trend that predates Elon), saying following someone should guarantee seeing their posts. He argues Twitter squishes complex issues onto one left-right axis and notes AI models (Claude, ChatGPT) tend toward consensus and are less polarizing than X or Bluesky.

quotes: Partisanship is the gravity that dictates every election in the US. 43 of the 50 states we could probably predict right now with 97% confidence who they vote for in 2028. And it's not because of rigging, it's because polarization and partisanship are very powerful forces.

I think California's system is completely unacceptable... the fact that the United States, the technological leader of the world, can't [count fast] is a sign of atrophy of the efficacy of the system.
It's not a good time to be a defender of the status quo.
I think it's insane that we had a candidate who was trying to be president until he was 86. And the alternative is someone who's trying to be president until he's 82.
summary.verdict_reason: Silver delivers concrete, numbers-backed election analysis and 2028 predictions relevant to anyone tracking political markets, though much of the first third is NBA/Knicks small talk that scanners can skip.

title: Socialists Sweep NYC, China Catches Up in Coding, AI Memory Crunch, Micron's Blowout Quarter

summary.tldr: With Freeberg out, guests Travis Kalanick and Gavin Baker join the crew to dissect the DSA's sweep of NYC Democratic primaries and its implications for the party, plus a deep dive into AI infrastructure economics. The strongest through-line: Chinese open-source models (GLM 5.2) have effectively caught up to US frontier models, memory (DRAM/HBM) is the binding bottleneck of the AI buildout, and orbital compute may become economically compelling as terrestrial data centers get more expensive.

topics.title: DSA sweeps NYC Democratic primaries
topics.detail: Mamdani went 3-for-3 on endorsements; Brad Lander unseated Dan Goldman in NY-10, Chevalier beat a five-term Jeffries-backed incumbent in NY-13, and Claire Valdez won NY-7. Polymarket had the Mamdani sweep at just 26% before election day. Baker argues the DSA's base is downwardly-mobile wealthy white liberals, not the working class, while Sacks lays out their platform (abolish ICE, the Senate, police/prisons, electoral college).

topics.title: Why socialism is gaining traction
topics.detail: Chamath frames AI as the 'greatest economic leveler' but says Silicon Valley has been terrible brand ambassadors, ceding the narrative to doomerism. Baker calls Mamdani 'one of the most talented politicians I've ever seen,' arguing the DSA is ascendant because of his charisma, not its ideas. Chamath praises Australia/UK/Canada's under-16 social media bans as a possible antidote to youth radicalization; Sacks counters that such bans are really a Trojan horse for de-anonymization and censorship.

topics.title: Chinese open-source models catch US frontier
topics.detail: Z.ai's GLM 5.2 (744B params, 1M token context, MIT license) scored 51 on the artificial analysis intelligence index — highest open-weight ever — trailing Opus 4.8 by under 1 point on SWE coding while being ~85% cheaper. Baker confirms heavy 'distillation' from US model APIs. Z.ai's founder claims the family was trained on Huawei Ascend 910B chips and that fable-level capability will arrive before Q1 2027.

topics.title: Anthropic's regulatory capture and the AI race
topics.detail: Sacks argues Dario Amodei essentially got what he wanted — a government approval process (his proposed 'FAA for AI') — after a jailbreak report led to Anthropic's Fable being rolled back and GPT-5.6 stuck in approval hoops. Sacks warns the US is 6 months ahead on models and 24 months ahead on silicon yet only 'a few months' ahead overall, calling self-imposed restrictions a losing game.

topics.title: Micron blowout and the DRAM bottleneck
topics.detail: Micron revenue up 4x YoY (guidance $50B vs $43B expected), stock up ~14x. Baker calls DRAM/HBM the single most important AI bottleneck and notes new supply-chain agreements have price floors above prior-cycle peak gross margins. Memory may be 30-40% of hyperscaler capex next year; consumer fallout includes Apple, Xbox and console price hikes ('AI-flation').

topics.title: Orbital compute economics and IPO dynamics
topics.detail: Baker breaks down a 1GW data center: ~$35B silicon + $25B power/cooling; if Starship launch drops below $25B (targeting ~$5B/GW when reusable), orbital 'racks in space linked with lasers' become compelling. On IPOs, Baker pegs Anthropic at ~$3T public value, explains Cerebras breaking deal price triggers forced price-insensitive selling by certain PMs, and notes SpaceX may lack a liquidity wall since holders sold every 6 months for a decade.

quotes: I think AI is the greatest economic leveler we'll ever find in our lifetime.
We're using the Democratic Party as a ballot access vehicle. Not because we share its goals.
This guy, Mamdani... he's got that Obama Trump charisma. And he is going to destroy your party from the inside out.
Making memory is... as close to magic as science can get.
Just picture Pentagon-sized data centers. No, it's racks in space, linked with lasers.
summary.verdict_reason: The AI infrastructure segments — Chinese open-source parity, the DRAM bottleneck, and orbital compute math — are genuinely substantive and numbers-rich, though the long political opening leans heavily editorial.

title: GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen's $56B Plan to Take Over eBay

summary.tldr: GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen sits for a long interview walking through his career from Chewy to GameStop and lays out his rationale for a $56B bid to acquire and run eBay. The core takeaway: Cohen wants to cut $2B in costs from eBay, build out live commerce and a marketplace for in-game digital items, and he's frustrated that eBay's board and CEO won't engage while the media dismisses GameStop as a meme stock.

topics.title: The Chewy playbook
topics.detail: Cohen switched from a planned online jewelry business to pet food because it was recurring/consumable revenue. He obsessed over supply-chain economics ('a game of pennies'), ran AdWords campaigns himself until 4-5am, and hired for 'will over skill.' He sold Chewy in 2017 for $3.35B and doesn't regret it despite it later IPOing at a far higher valuation.

topics.title: GameStop turnaround and numbers
topics.detail: Cohen started as a passive investor on a console-cycle thesis around 2020, then became CEO. His initial mistake was applying the Chewy e-commerce playbook; he pivoted to cost-cutting and collectibles (TCG, sports cards, PSA-graded trade-ins). Cited metrics: collectibles now 42% of revenue, Q1 revenue $835M up 14% YoY, SG&A cut from $228M to $202M, $9.7B cash, $333M free cash flow.

topics.title: The eBay bid and thesis
topics.detail: Cohen is offering 50% cash / 50% GameStop stock at a premium (total ~$56B). He argues eBay has stagnated post-Donahoe: GMV down, active users down ~30M, opex over half of revenue with $2.4B on marketing for no user growth. He'd cut $2B in costs and treat sellers (the real customers) far better.

topics.title: Growth vectors: live commerce and digital collectibles
topics.detail: Cohen wants eBay to dominate live commerce (a ~$400B TAM currently being 'crushed' by a large competitor), using 1,600 GameStop stores as creator studios and fulfillment nodes. His unannounced idea: build a liquidity marketplace for in-game digital items (skins, weapons), which he says has real utility unlike NFTs and could dwarf eBay's physical marketplace.

topics.title: Board resistance and next steps
topics.detail: eBay's board rejected the offer citing financing uncertainty; the CEO and board won't take meetings, and a vote to lower the special-meeting threshold from 20% to 10% failed narrowly. Cohen notes the CEO has a ~$100M golden parachute, says he's putting $500M of his own money in, and vows he 'won't go away' — signaling a possible hostile route or tender.

topics.title: Grievance with the media and short sellers
topics.detail: Cohen repeatedly argues everyone 'wants GameStop to fail,' contrasting his own capital at risk with an overpaid management team and board that 'don't buy stock with their own money.' Host Chamath theorizes the media can't credit Cohen without admitting they were wrong to call GameStop a meme stock.

quotes: I was on the toilet
If our suppliers are sending us gifts in the mail, that's a really bad sign — it means we're overpaying.
I look for will over skill... basically be as psychotic as me, and psychopaths attract psychopaths.
There's nothing more American than basically risking your own capital. So why does everyone want us to fail?
The market is a voting machine in the short term but a weighing machine in the long term.
summary.verdict_reason: A rare, candid CEO interview with concrete operating lessons and a detailed strategic case for a live $56B takeover battle, though it's one-sided and light on hard financing details.

title: World's First Trillionaire, Anthropic Fable Banned, The New Oligarchs, Iran Peace Deal

summary.tldr: The All-In hosts spend most of the episode on ideological monologues about wealth, agency, and "makers vs. takers," framed around SpaceX's record trillion-dollar IPO. The meatiest content is David Sacks' insider readout on why the White House shut down Anthropic's 'Fable' model over a jailbreak dispute, plus a discussion of a supposed Iran peace deal. Central takeaway: the AI frontier labs' fear-mongering and evasiveness are handing hyperscalers a regulatory-capture opportunity.

topics.title: SpaceX IPO and cursor acquisition
topics.detail: SpaceX IPO'd at $135/share (up to $177 by taping), briefly hitting ~$2T market cap and passing Amazon/Microsoft; it raised ~$85B, roughly 3x Saudi Aramco's 2019 IPO. SpaceX does ~$19B revenue vs Amazon's $717B. It acquired cursor (doing $4B revenue) for ~$15B in stock; Chamath argues Elon effectively got a 50% discount via a stock-for-stock deal as SpaceX's valuation rises, and predicts a future Tesla/SpaceX merger.

topics.title: Wealth, agency and 'makers vs. takers'
topics.detail: Sacks argues Elon has 'not one more dollar' post-IPO—wealth is the market's discounted value of the 'machines that make stuff.' Chamath recounts growing up on ~$17-19k/yr welfare in Canada, using it to argue government support creates learned helplessness. Freeberg reframes society as 'makers vs. takers,' calling politicians/intelligentsia (Warren, Sanders, Khanna) the takers building an 'American Politburo.'

topics.title: Anthropic 'Fable' model shutdown
topics.detail: Commerce Secretary Lutnick ordered Anthropic to restrict Fable (guardrailed version of 'mythos') to US citizens; Anthropic couldn't/wouldn't comply granularly and shut it down. Sacks says Anthropic's own partner (reportedly Amazon) found a serious jailbreak, escalated to the White House, Treasury Secretary called Dario personally, and Dario refused to take it down—doubling down in a blog post distinguishing 'good' vs 'bad' jailbreaks. Anthropic had also allegedly over-shared mythos to ~50 companies including SK Telecom.

topics.title: AI regulatory capture and centralization
topics.detail: Chamath argues frontier lab leaders' doomerism and immaturity are creating an opening for hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google) to become KYC-enforcing gatekeepers, killing the neocloud market and open ecosystem. Sacks claims Anthropic's philosophy treats competition as a 'dangerous race condition' and wants a government-blessed cartel; notes Biden's AI officials went to work at Anthropic post-administration.

topics.title: Iran 'peace deal'
topics.detail: Sacks praises a proposed MOU: reopening Strait of Hormuz, Iran giving up enriched uranium under IAEA supervision, freezing nuclear program 60 days, in exchange for lifted sanctions and $300B reconstruction (not US-funded). Chamath disagrees, calling the war a 'huge blunder' and arguing Israel's 'trim the grass' containment approach was better than full-scale war.

quotes: He doesn't have one more dollar in the bank than he did the day before the IPO... it's that other people are placing a greater value on what he's built.
The lie is that the rich are unfairly rich and the poor are unfairly poor... The truth is that there are two sides that are the makers and the takers.
You've built a machine that outputs 'me' no matter what you feed it... The more precise term is epistemic exceptionalism.
They literally walk themselves into prison and hand the hyperscalers the keys.
summary.verdict_reason: The Anthropic/Fable insider readout from Sacks is genuinely substantive for anyone tracking AI regulation, but it's buried under repetitive ideological monologues and a thinly-sourced Iran segment.

title: Anthropic's Fable Backlash, Nationalizing AI, Inflation Heats Up & California's Broken Elections

summary.tldr: The besties dissect Anthropic's new 'Fable 5' model release, which introduced mandatory 30-day prompt retention and silently downgraded users doing frontier AI research or asking sensitive science questions—sparking a developer backlash and accusations of regulatory capture. They debate whether the fear-mongering is genuine safety concern or an anti-competitive push to ban open-source models (which are increasingly Chinese), then cover Bernie Sanders' proposal to seize 50% of AI companies, a hot inflation print, and alleged election irregularities in the LA mayoral primary. The through-line: AI's guardrails may push US companies toward Chinese open-source models and consolidate power in one or two labs.

topics.title: Anthropic's Fable 5 backlash
topics.detail: The model tops benchmarks but costs ~2x Opus 4.8 per token. It retains all prompts/context for 30 days with no exceptions (even zero-retention enterprise contracts), and silently downgraded users doing AI/ML/chip-design research or asking benign science questions—Ben Thompson got kicked out asking about cancer and GLP-1s. Anthropic walked back only the non-disclosure of downgrades. Jason live-tested it and got downgraded twice on-air, once for a fertilizer-regulation question.

topics.title: Regulatory capture and the open-source flight to China
topics.detail: Sacks claims Anthropic is running a fear-mongering regulatory-capture campaign, and Dario's new blog calls for an FAA/FDA-style agency to approve all models—which Sacks argues is a preemptive strike to ban open-source. Freeberg says restrictions are forcing his genomics company (Ohalo) to run local open-source models, and 'the best open source models today are Chinese.' He cites using the Arc Institute's genome language model funded by the Collisons.

topics.title: Chamath's data-center bet
topics.detail: Chamath says a gigawatt data center now costs ~$100B (up ~20x from $4-5B when he started), and he's considering building 2-3 gigawatts on Arizona land specifically to enable liquid access to open-source models, rather than flipping to Blackstone/Google.

topics.title: Bernie Sanders wants half of AI companies
topics.detail: Sanders' 'American AI Sovereign Wealth Fund Act' proposes a one-time 50% tax on the stock of the largest AI firms (OpenAI, Anthropic, XAI). Sacks opposes it as confiscation but sympathizes since these public-benefit corporations trained free on humanity's knowledge while predicting mass job loss. Freeberg counters that Social Security should be restructured into an equity-owning sovereign wealth fund with individual accounts.

topics.title: The AI jobs debate
topics.detail: Freeberg insists there is no AI job loss—it's a revenue-side productivity boom; he's adding 15 engineering headcount. Sacks distinguishes the CEOs' claims: Elon (utopian post-scarcity), Dario (50% entry-level knowledge-worker loss in 1-5 years, already refuted), Sam (walked it back). May jobs report: 172K new jobs, 4.3% unemployment.

topics.title: Hot inflation print
topics.detail: May CPI came in at 4.2% YoY (highest since April 2023) and PPI at 6.5% (highest since end of 2022), largely attributed to the Iran war energy spike. Chamath warns oil could hit $150-200/barrel if China runs out of reserves and re-enters spot markets, and calls for off-ramping the Iran conflict. Markets were up because the print was roughly in line with expectations.

topics.title: LA mayoral primary election dispute
topics.detail: Spencer Pratt led in-person voting (35%) but late-arriving mail ballots swung ~80% toward Nithya Raman, concentrated around Skid Row. Freeberg argues California's laws (unlimited ballot harvesting, universal mailed ballots, no voter ID) make it a system of 'appointment, not election'—legal fraud. Sacks calls it crooked; Jason pushes back that no modern major election has been swung by fraud and demands Trump's DOJ investigate.

quotes: One thing I'll say about anthropic is they tell the truth. It's just that the truth sucks when you actually take it and you eat it.
The American open source models are not as good as the Chinese open source models.
If you're building Frankenstein... stop making Frankenstein, unplug it. You are in charge of this monster, but you're acting as if you can't stop the monster while you're building the monster.
There is no election. Your rights to have an election are gone. You are a citizen of those who tell you who your overseers are.
She had a better cheating operation, obviously.
summary.verdict_reason: The Anthropic segment offers a genuinely sharp, specific analysis of AI guardrails driving companies toward Chinese open-source models plus concrete numbers on data-center costs, though the lengthy election argument is more heat than insight.

title: All-In's Best Ideas Pitch Competition: 4 Investors Present Their Top Trades Live

summary.tldr: A live pitch competition at an All-In event where four professional investors each present a single high-conviction trade idea, followed by Q&A and judging from the hosts. The four picks: MGM (casino operator, Aaron Cowen), Talen Energy (nuclear/gas power producer, Dan Dreyfus), Actinium/Actus Oncology (radiopharma biotech, Oleg Nodelman), and GEODNET (crypto RTK-precision-location network, Kyle Samani). MGM won both audience and bestie votes on its capped-downside, multi-catalyst thesis.

topics.title: MGM Resorts (Aaron Cowen, Surretta Capital)
topics.detail: Cowen argues MGM is a triple, driven not by Vegas but by hidden Asian/Middle East optionality: an Osaka, Japan casino license opening 2030 (est. $2B EBITDA, 40% owned) targeting Chinese gamblers, plus a Dubai property built with 300k sq ft of empty space for future legalized gambling. Barry Diller owns 26% and bid $48 (stock rose from ~$37); Cowen says don't tender, sees the stock worth $60 base, ~$50 more for Japan, $40-50 more for Dubai, potentially $100-150.

topics.title: Talen Energy (Dan Dreyfus)
topics.detail: A power producer with 2GW nuclear + 6GW gas trading at ~$25B EV vs ~$45B replacement cost (Sam Zell playbook). Stock in high $300s at ~7x free cash flow; base case doubles to ~$50/share FCF doing nothing, ~$70 with more data-center contracts (15x = ~$1,050), $100+ if they build new capacity. Thesis: US must copy China's power buildout; PJM region needs 106GW over 10 years. Cited Microsoft's Three Mile Island deal at $100/MWh for 20 years.

topics.title: Actus Oncology / radiopharma (Oleg Nodelman, EcoR1 Capital)
topics.detail: Radiopharmaceuticals pitched as 'microdrones' with 100-micron blast radius. Actus (AKTS): ~$1B market cap, ~$500M enterprise value, 3+ years cash, IPO 18x oversubscribed with $100M Lilly backstop. Targets Nectin-IV (bladder) and B7H3 (major solid tumors), data expected Q1 2027. Actinium payload sourced from US-only radium-233 waste, creating a moat against China generics. Nodelman claims potential $10B / $200 per share if one program hits.

topics.title: GEODNET (Kyle Samani, ex-Multicoin)
topics.detail: A crypto-incentivized RTK network giving ~2cm location precision (100x GPS), with ~22,000 base stations (2x the next three legacy players combined), live in 150 countries. Customers include John Deere, DJI, TomTom. ~$11M ARR growing 3x YoY; 80% of revenue (~$8.8M) buys GEO tokens on the open market. Fully diluted ~$150M valuation. Token GEOD trades on Solana; Samani says buy the token, not equity.

topics.title: Judging and sizing debate
topics.detail: Chamath framed picks by asymmetric alpha vs downside and liquidity, noting he couldn't get $1M into GEODNET without moving the market, while MGM and Talen could absorb tens of millions. Gavin Baker ranked MGM > Talen > Actus, praising MGM's Diller-bid-capped downside and worrying about AI regulatory risk hitting Talen. Freeberg noted MGM's undermonetized entertainment (sphere shows adding ~$1M/day EBITDA). MGM won both audience (50%) and bestie votes.

quotes: I said, Amazon's going to be a trillion dollar company. And I was laughed out of the room. David Einhorn... said, I know trillion dollar companies, this is not a trillion dollar company, wrong.
If I had just picked NVIDIA, I would have been an IRIS legend that I could have retired.
We do not need AI demand to keep the power markets incredibly tight for the next 20 years. AI demand just turbocharges.
In a sector where virtually everyone else is a momentum investor betting on science, we focus on margin of safety... we want to monetize other kids' science projects.
I feel about crypto exactly the way I do about snowboarding... please don't go sideways down the mountain and ruin the powder, David.
summary.verdict_reason: Four concrete, well-argued single-stock/token theses with real numbers and pushback from the judges make it a useful listen for investors, even if the entertainment-heavy framing and self-serving pitches warrant skepticism.

title: Senators John Fetterman and Dave McCormick: Bipartisanship, Money in DC, Datacenters, Graham Platner

summary.tldr: Pennsylvania Senators John Fetterman (D) and Dave McCormick (R) appear together at an All-In event to model bipartisanship, focusing heavily on why AI data centers and energy buildout are an economic boom for Pennsylvania rather than a 'scourge.' The central takeaway: both senators argue that opposition to data centers is fueled partly by Chinese-linked misinformation and that a US moratorium would be a 'China first' policy handing the AI race to Beijing. Fetterman repeatedly breaks with his own party on Israel, AI, and 'Trump Derangement Syndrome.'

topics.title: Data centers as a blue-collar boom
topics.detail: McCormick cites his July Energy Innovation Summit with $92B of committed investment, the Homer City coal-to-gas conversion (4.4 GW, 3.4 GW to a data center complex, 1 GW back to grid), and claims seasoned welders/electricians in their early 20s make over $100k with 25-30% wage increases. He estimates ~2 logistics jobs per data center job and four 'buckets' of economic activity.

topics.title: AI moratorium framed as pro-China
topics.detail: Fetterman says a Democratic push for a data-center moratorium is 'a China first' policy and warns the US is only 'six to eight months ahead of China.' Both invoke the fracking/shale analogy: 15 years of misinformation gave way to ~80% support, and McCormick claims current opposition is 'largely being driven by China and outside forces.'

topics.title: Fetterman breaks with his party
topics.detail: Fetterman defends the filibuster (admitting Democrats were 'so wrong' to want it gone in 2020-22), supports holding Iran accountable even at $5/gallon gas, backs Israel, and refuses to call opponents Nazis or fascists. He says he votes the Democratic line ~93% of the time and accepts he could be primaried.

topics.title: Graham Platner and the Democratic 'lurch left'
topics.detail: Fetterman attacks Maine candidate Graham Platner for a Nazi tattoo, alleged sexting with a dozen women, and calling a US soldier a 'dumb motherfucker that doesn't deserve to live' and the Army 'absolute trash.' McCormick says the rise of socialism and anti-Semitism on the left is 'horribly unhealthy,' while Jason Calacanis blames Trump's McCain 'not a war hero' remark for the rhetoric.

topics.title: Inequality, capitalism, and money in politics
topics.detail: With Pennsylvania median income at $52,000, the hosts press on wealth concentration; Fetterman warns 'we are going to lose capitalism' without solutions and touts Invest America accounts and $1,700 school-choice contributions. Fetterman's race cost ~$500M ($200M his side, $300M opposition); McCormick's 2022 race was ~$330M, and both expect 2026/2028 to dwarf that.

quotes: "If Democrats run me out of the party, they will be attacking a committed Democrat that votes your line 93% of the time."
"Do you want to give that race to China? ... Do you want America to build that chassis? Or do you want the Chinese to do that?"
"I am a beneficiary of capitalism ... But we are going to lose capitalism unless we find a combination of ways to address it."
"Trump comes out for ice cream and lazy Sundays. We're going to hate that. And we're going to vote it down."
summary.verdict_reason: The data-center-as-economic-engine argument and specific Pennsylvania energy/AI investment numbers are genuinely useful for tech and markets readers, even if the political banter and transcription glitches make parts hard to follow.

title: Dan Dreyfus: America's Critical Minerals Crisis is Here

summary.tldr: Investor Dan Dreyfus (Bornite Capital) delivers a presentation arguing that the US faces a severe critical-minerals and infrastructure crisis just as multiple capital cycles (AI data centers, defense, aerospace, grid, semiconductors) create a massive demand shock colliding with decades of supply underinvestment. His central takeaway: copper and other critical minerals are the next great bottleneck, China dominates supply and processing, and hard assets are the way to protect purchasing power against currency debasement.

topics.title: The end of capital-light growth
topics.detail: Dreyfus argues the 2000s-2020s US 'economic miracle' created trillions in market cap with almost no capital (Google, Meta buying WhatsApp for $30B with 12 employees, SaaS, Apple) while the US tore down industrial infrastructure and moved it to China, leaving supply chains fragile and causing persistent post-COVID inflation.

topics.title: Simultaneous capital cycles straining minerals
topics.detail: He lists overlapping trillion-dollar cycles: aerospace (Boeing/Airbus $1T backlog over 10 years plus space economy), grid modernization (a 106-year-old California power line caused the Paradise fire killing ~300), power generation, data centers (~$1T/year), semi-fabs ($750B, likely trillions), and defense—all dependent on critical minerals.

topics.title: China's export cutoff and the government response
topics.detail: Last April China cut off exports of materials including samarium, gadolinium, terbium, dysprosium, scandium, yttrium; Ford was reportedly within days of shutting production over samarium-cobalt magnets. The DoD/DoE are now offering small resource owners a three-part package: an equity check, an expedited permit, and a take-or-pay offtake agreement with a floor price.

topics.title: Copper as the next bottleneck
topics.detail: A 1GW AI data center needs 50,000 tons of copper; building 15GW/year means 750,000 tons just for data centers, versus total copper supply growth of only 500,000 tons last year. Growing at GDP means needing 700M tons over 18 years—as much as mined in all of human history—requiring five tier-one mines online yearly when almost none are coming; mines take 7-12 years to build.

topics.title: Currency debasement and the 1970s parallel
topics.detail: US has $40T federal debt growing $2.5T/year plus ~$100T of social liabilities also growing $2.5T/year, against only $5.5T tax receipts. Dreyfus predicts massive money printing in the next recession, comparing to the 1970s when the dollar lost 70% of purchasing power and commodities/hard assets were the best-performing asset class.

topics.title: Energy, labor, and the processing problem
topics.detail: A 1GW solar-powered data center needs 5GW of solar across 35,000 acres (bigger than San Francisco). Craft labor is the biggest bottleneck; Quanta trainees start at $150K out of high school. Rare earths are abundant but China holds the processing know-how; silver faces a ~200M oz annual deficit with only ~600M oz inventory—roughly three years left.

quotes: China leading over us and squeezing our testicles every time that we don't do something that they don't like
Over the next 18 years, we're going to need as much copper as we mined in the last 10,000 years.
It's truly what I call a vuja de moment, which is the overwhelming feeling that none of this has ever happened before.
A lot of urban Americans still think a ham sandwich comes from the refrigerator.
summary.verdict_reason: It's a dense, specific, number-heavy investment thesis on critical minerals and the physical bottlenecks behind the AI buildout—valuable for market-minded listeners, though it's essentially a one-sided sales pitch with little pushback.

title: Bill Maris: How Google Could Crush AI Competitors, Why Small Funds Win, and AI's Atari Stage

summary.tldr: Bill Maris, founder of Google Ventures and now Section 32, lays out four career lessons and argues small funds (sub-$750M) mathematically outperform large ones. The most provocative thread: Google could crush OpenAI and Anthropic simply by slashing token prices ~80%, and today's AI mega-companies are dumping overpriced late-stage risk onto public 401(k) holders while claiming to serve humanity. He sees AI at the 'Atari command-line stage' and prefers investing in the infrastructure beneath models rather than the models themselves.

topics.title: Small funds beat large funds (the math)
topics.detail: Maris claims top-decile funds under $750M average 4.76x DPI vs 2.42x for funds over $1B, and sub-$750M funds represent 95% of top-decile performers. A $7B fund needs to return $210B in exits at 3x, exceeding total venture-backed M&A/IPO value in most years.

topics.title: How Google could crush OpenAI and Anthropic
topics.detail: Maris argues if he ran Google he'd arbitrarily cut token prices 80%, and since customers can get a near-identical product from Gemini for 80% less, competitors face 'super critical' margin pressure. He notes the counterpoint that OpenAI/Anthropic could burn investor cash Uber-style to grab share—citing '$1.4 trillion in spend commitments on $60 billion of revenue.'

topics.title: Retail as the bag holder
topics.detail: Maris objects to companies wrapping themselves in 'public benefit' language while keeping value creation to elite investors, then forcing overpriced late-stage products onto 401(k)s and ETFs via 'bizarre exceptions to S&P 500 rules.' He says humanity needs money, so these firms should go public sooner.

topics.title: AI's 'Atari stage' and infrastructure bets
topics.detail: Maris compares AI today to 1980s text games like Zork—brittle, with memory loss and session resets—predicting a jump to 'PlayStation 10' quality in five years. He won't invest in larger models; he wants the 'controllers, physics engines and GPUs'—the platform machinery underneath.

topics.title: Biotech, longevity, and science flight offshore
topics.detail: Maris (founder of Calico, investor in Flatiron, Veer, New Limit) is interested in computational biology but avoids therapeutics requiring human clinical trials. He argues gutting the CDC/NIH, anti-science sentiment, and pushing out H1B holders is driving scientific mindshare to China, which is recruiting top scientists from Europe and India.

topics.title: Broken venture incentives
topics.detail: Sacks and Maris dissect why large funds distort the market: a $5B fund returning 1.01x still claims 75th percentile and out-earns a $500M fund returning 3x. Giant funds inflate valuations (offering a founder $250M at $4B instead of $20M at $100M), which founders take unless seasoned.

quotes: To see the future sometimes you need to be a little bit insane. It may appear to those around you that you are tarring the roof in the thunderstorm.
Don't bet against computer science... if you apply the right kind of computer science at the right time to the right problem, you will get to the right answers.
We're going to force overpriced products on the 401k holders of America who didn't get to participate early.
I think we're at the Atari command-line stage of AI and we're going to get to the PlayStation 10 stage in the next five years.
If I'm Google and I decide to arbitrarily cut the cost of tokens 80%... what happens to the business models of OpenAI and Anthropic?
summary.verdict_reason: Maris offers concrete, data-backed contrarian arguments on fund size, Google's competitive leverage, and the risk being passed to retail investors—substantive enough to reward listeners despite the format being a conference talk plus Q&A.

title: Nikesh Arora: Mythos is Real, Analytical SaaS is Dead, and Google can be a $10T company

summary.tldr: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora joins All-In to discuss AI's impact on cybersecurity and enterprise software. His central claims: AI-powered vulnerability detection ('Mythos') is real and dangerous, 'analytical SaaS is dead,' and Google will be the first $10 trillion company. The most important takeaway is that AI radically accelerates both cyber offense and defense while collapsing the value of data-analysis SaaS layers.

topics.title: Mythos vulnerability detection is real
topics.detail: Arora says AI found in six weeks the code vulnerabilities that normally took Palo Alto five to seven years to find, at a cost in the low millions of dollars in tokens. But the false positive rate was ~30%, making it 'great for attack, horrible for defense.' He predicts these capabilities will be available in open-source/Chinese models (GLM 4.6, etc.) within three months, not the six previously assumed.

topics.title: Analytical SaaS is dead
topics.detail: Arora argues analytical SaaS—companies that collect and analyze your data for you—is 'over,' because you can now run models directly against your own data. Jason gives an example of cutting a SaaS bill 90% by dropping 17 of 20 seats and connecting the data to Slack and Claude. Infrastructure software (Databricks, Snowflake, MongoDB, Oracle) is undervalued because enterprises will need 10x more stored data within three years.

topics.title: UI death and re-engineering systems of work
topics.detail: Arora calls enterprise/consumer UI 'the worst thing we did as technologists.' If agents can interact directly with backends, UI goes away and systems of work/record get re-engineered over the next five years, enabling '5 people become 1' efficiency.

topics.title: Google as the first $10T company
topics.detail: Arora calls Google underrated and predicts it becomes the first $10 trillion company, citing that model companies still need salesforces and the three hyperscalers have the most salespeople. He argues profit pools sit in the application layer—which is why OpenAI and Anthropic are pushing there—but believes a new layer of AI application companies is still unformed.

topics.title: National security and economic chaos risk
topics.detail: 89% of breaches come from stolen credentials, not sophisticated cracking. Arora is less worried about big infrastructure (PG&E) and more about small dentist/doctor offices and events like the Change Healthcare ransomware attack. Notably, a model CEO told him the entire weights of their newest frontier model fit on a USB stick and can be distilled in 24-48 hours.

topics.title: M&A strategy and hardware
topics.detail: Palo Alto ran a playbook of buying product companies to feed its go-to-market engine to reach ~$150B, then made a $25B identity acquisition (closed three months ago). Arora targets 90s gross margin and 40s net margin via AI-driven operational efficiency. He believes hardware persists (financial services can't tolerate cloud latency) and expects to hire MORE technical people, not fewer, due to AI transformation.

quotes: In six weeks we found vulnerabilities which have normally taken us five to seven years to find.
If you're an analytical SaaS company, it's over.
UI enterprise software and consumer software UI is the worst thing we did as technologists.
The entire model weights of their newest model fits on a USB stick.
I think it's going to be the first $10 trillion company in our lifetime.
summary.verdict_reason: Arora offers concrete, contrarian claims on AI cybersecurity, the SaaS shakeout, and enterprise value creation that are directly useful for tech and markets readers, despite the fawning 'armchair CEO' detour.

title: Inside the Private Stock Market Boom: SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI & the Rise of Secondaries

summary.tldr: An All-In live panel with Gavin Baker (Atreides), Brad Gerstner (Altimeter), and Kelly Rodriques (Forge CEO) on the explosion of private-market secondaries for late-stage giants like SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI. The core takeaway: secondaries now rival IPOs and M&A as the primary exit path, retail access is opening up via a Schwab-Forge deal, and the panelists warn retail not to YOLO into fully-valued names at the top of the cycle.

topics.title: The secondary market boom by the numbers
topics.detail: Secondary transaction volume is now roughly double the 2021 peak. Employee/investor secondaries into names like SpaceX, Anthropic, and OpenAI represent 31% of all primary venture activity in 2025. Pricing flipped from an ~80-cents-on-the-dollar discount to a ~106% premium (Q1), not counting predatory SPVs charging 10% load fees and double carry.

topics.title: Why companies stay private — and whether it's good
topics.detail: Gerstner and Baker argue there's no genuinely good reason to stay private; founders just avoid public-market scrutiny. Baker cites Zuckerberg's claim that had Facebook been public during the apps-vs-HTML5 debate (Chamath's apps position vs. Bret Taylor's HTML5, which Zuck initially backed), rigorous public investors would have corrected the mistake faster. They call private markets 'sycophantic' because investors flatter founders to keep access.

topics.title: The Schwab-Forge deal and democratizing access
topics.detail: Rodriques frames the Forge-Schwab deal as legitimizing private companies as an asset class, adding 46 million Schwab investors and $12 trillion. Interval funds now hold ~60 companies (including SpaceX) with $500 minimums for unaccredited investors. Forge got SPVs on SpaceX permissioned by Elon back in 2018-19; the pitch to founders is broad retail distribution ahead of IPO.

topics.title: Retail risk and 'exit liquidity' warnings
topics.detail: Gerstner says he told CNBC he'd deploy only ~30 of a hypothetical $100k today, not all, after two of the biggest public-market months in a decade. He warns retail against YOLOing into levered SPVs and the 14 levered ETFs launching on the SpaceX IPO day (~$1.75T valuation). Closed-end funds are called a 'FOMO' bet with underlying values disconnected from reality.

topics.title: VC franchise risk and coming institutional demand
topics.detail: Baker warns VC firms without exposure to a trillion-dollar company face 'franchise risk,' pushing them into 'unnatural' bets like writing call options on neo-labs. He predicts hundreds of billions in new late-stage demand as long-only funds (Fidelity, Baillie Gifford, Wellington, T. Rowe), currently capped at self-imposed 3-7% private limits, free up capacity when companies IPO and shares move out of the private bucket.

topics.title: Under-the-radar names they'd buy
topics.detail: Gerstner: Sierra (Bret Taylor's agent-native Salesforce) and Parloa. Baker: Revolut (neo-bank), plus public networking plays Arista and DriveNets riding an inference 'disaggregation' super-cycle. Rodriques: Neura Robotics (Germany, ~$100M revenue). Chamath (host): VAST (space stations) and Zipline drone delivery, which cut maternal mortality 90-95% in African villages before coming to the US.

quotes: The sycophantic nature of private markets is real.
99 was Vegas on a Friday night after way too many drugs... CMGI had no revenue and the stock went from $2 to $2,000 over the course of six months.
Venture capitalists don't think about the sell part. They think about the buy part.
We may not be at the top, but we ain't at the bottom. We're bouncing along the top.
summary.verdict_reason: Concrete data on secondary-market mechanics plus candid, experienced takes on valuations, retail risk, and specific investable names make it useful for anyone tracking private tech markets, even if the mutual-admiration tone runs thick.

title: The IPO Comeback: Why Tech Giants Are Finally Going Public | All-In Liquidity IPO Panel

summary.tldr: A live All-In panel moderated by Jason Calacanis and Brad Gerstner featuring Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman and Planet Labs CEO Will Marshall on their IPO experiences and the trends behind AI silicon and space data. The core takeaway: the IPO window is reopening, and Gerstner argues the pendulum is swinging back toward companies going public earlier so public-market investors can capture upside instead of it all accruing to private markets.

topics.title: What going public actually changes (and doesn't)
topics.detail: Feldman argues the process involves enormous 'garbage'—Zoom calls with 130 attendees, endless document reviews—and that the morning after the IPO 'you've sold no more stuff' and engineering hasn't progressed. Cerebras priced at $185 (range raised twice), opened around $320, and trades near $230 for a ~$50-60B market cap; the timing looked easy only after 'nine and a half years of really hard.'

topics.title: Planet Labs' business and space data
topics.detail: Marshall's stock went from ~$5 to ~$50 (10x) since going public via SPAC at ~$2B in 2021. Planet runs ~200 satellites imaging the entire Earth daily; security/military is now ~60% of revenue, higher than they'd initially guessed. He credits ~4-5x lower launch costs plus satellite miniaturization (a former billion-dollar, 20-ton satellite now costs a few kilograms).

topics.title: Space data centers debate
topics.detail: Marshall claims when launch costs fall to $200-300/kg (currently ~$1,000, down 10x in a decade) it becomes cheaper to put data centers in space, powered by sun-synchronous solar panels gathering 5x more energy with no batteries needed. Planet is launching Google TPUs and has already launched NVIDIA GPUs to space. Feldman is skeptical, noting unsolved problems in building communicating clusters in space and comparing it to self-driving's brutal 'last 10%.'

topics.title: Cerebras' contrarian silicon bet
topics.detail: Feldman argues if you want to be 20x better you can't copy NVIDIA's GPU architecture. Cerebras built a dinner-plate-sized chip (vs postage-stamp) putting memory next to compute, claiming 15-18x faster than a GPU for customers like OpenAI. His framing: nobody waits for slow AI—'How big is the market for dial-up? Zero.'

topics.title: The case for IPO-ing earlier
topics.detail: Gerstner argues more money is historically made after IPO than before, citing MongoDB (Altimeter distributed shares at ~$3-4B, then it hit $50B) and Planet, where 90% of value was created years post-IPO with early VCs like Google and Capricorn holding on. He says the 'stay private forever' Andreessen-era pendulum is swinging back, and Feldman would have preferred going public 18 months earlier at ~$10B vs $50B.

topics.title: IPO structure innovation
topics.detail: Gerstner highlights a 'dribble lockup' where shares release over six months tied to performance hurdles, which he expects SpaceX to adopt. He also notes SpaceX would need 'quadrillion-dollar valuations' to deliver the same post-IPO multiple that companies going public at a few billion historically achieved.

quotes: A lot of people asked us about how we got the timing right. And I think the answer is by getting it wrong for a decade.
Everything was really hard until it got really easy.
If you build a GPU, the odds that you're better than NVIDIA in our view are approximately zero.
How big is the market for slow search today? It's zero. How big is the market for dial-up? It's zero. You will not wait for AI.
All the cool stuff we're doing with LLMs is based on just the text of the internet... they don't know shit about the real world. I call them blind.
summary.verdict_reason: Two founders and a top tech investor deliver concrete, numbers-backed takes on the IPO reopening, AI silicon architecture, and space compute, though the format is promotional and light on genuine disagreement beyond the space data center exchange.

title: Dan Loeb: The Lost Art of Short Selling, and Why Stock Picking is Back

summary.tldr: Activist investor Dan Loeb (CEO/CIO of Third Point, ~$30B AUM) talks through his evolution from anonymous 1990s internet stock-message-board 'troll' and short-seller into a multi-strategy platform investor. The core investing takeaway: the era of ignoring technology or macro is over — everything is now correlated to the tech through-line, short selling is back as a live opportunity, and stock picking (plus credit picking) matters again. He also makes a bullish case on NVIDIA and closes with his work on criminal justice reform and the Ross Ulbricht pardon.

topics.title: Origins as an online short-seller
topics.detail: Loeb was active on 1990s chat boards (Yahoo, Silicon Investor) before Reddit/WallStreetBets existed, calling himself the 'OT' (original troll). He describes exposing fraudulent 1990s companies like Actrade, a factoring company run by a repeat fraudster that repackaged factored securities under fake 'TAD' technology while trading at a large multiple of book.

topics.title: How Third Point's style evolved
topics.detail: Loeb learned valuation at Warburg Pincus, risk-arb, and distressed debt at Jefferies (his '10,000 reps'), covering clients like David Tepper.
